question: Based on Figure 5.1-2 and Figure 5.1-3, is the RCS instrumentation layout adequate for monitoring natural circulation conditions?

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation, eliminating the need for pumps or external piping, and is integrated within each NuScale Power Module. The RCS is designed to safely transfer heat from the reactor core, maintain proper coolant chemistry and boron concentration, and ensure fuel integrity under normal and transient conditions. These features support both operational efficiency and safety for each module in the plant.

nuscale_ch05_0000.title: Preface
nuscale_ch05_0000.summary: The Preface outlines the copyright and usage restrictions for the NuScale Standard Plant Design Certification Application, specifically Chapter Five on the Reactor Coolant System and Connecting Systems. It specifies that only the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) is authorized to use, copy, or disclose the information for regulatory purposes, and any copies must retain the copyright notice.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. It removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for removing noncondensible gases and maintaining pressure boundary integrity to prevent radionuclide release. The RCS interfaces with several other systems, including emergency core cooling and chemical control, to ensure safe and efficient operation.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and transient conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) does not use traditional components with designed leakage rates, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using installed instrumentation. All leakage into containment is treated as unidentified until the source is determined, with leakage rates monitored through RCS inventory balance and containment evacuation system (CES) flow rates, and operational limits set in technical specifications. The leakage detection systems are designed to be reliable, redundant, and sensitive to support safety analyses.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, without relying on external power. It ensures the reactor coolant system temperature drops below 420°F within 36 hours, maintaining fuel and pressure boundary safety limits, and its reliability and risk significance are rigorously evaluated. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section describes the testing and inspection requirements for the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) components in the NuScale reactor, ensuring compliance with ASME codes for Class 2 components. It details how DHRS actuation valves are exercised during cold shutdown conditions, outlines the required testing procedures, and specifies that an in-situ heat removal test will be performed on the first installed reactor module as part of the initial test program.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components in the NuScale reactor, specifying types of steel and nickel-based alloys, as well as their compliance with relevant BPVC (Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code) material specifications. It also references where further descriptions of specific components can be found in other sections of the report.

edges.description: Section A provides a technical summary of the RCS design and its reliance on natural circulation, which is the subject matter addressed in the broader context introduced by Section B's preface to the chapter.

Section A provides a general overview of the RCS's reliance on natural circulation, while Section B specifies system features (like degasification and venting) that support the RCS's operational integrity, both of which are relevant to evaluating the adequacy of instrumentation for monitoring natural circulation.

Section A and Section B are identical text excerpts describing the RCS's reliance on natural circulation, providing the design context necessary to assess the adequacy of instrumentation layouts for monitoring such conditions.

Section A is a preface providing publication and copyright information for the NuScale Standard Plant Design Certification Application, while Section B gives a technical summary of the Reactor Coolant System's features, so Section A structurally references or introduces the context for the detailed content found in Section B.

Section A describes specific features of the RCS, such as degasification and venting, that support the general design basis in Section B, which states that the RCS relies on natural circulation without pumps.

Section B explains that the NuScale RCS relies on natural circulation without pumps, while Section A discusses the limitations of installed instrumentation in detecting RCS leakage, connecting them through the context of how the system's design (natural circulation) impacts the adequacy of instrumentation for monitoring such conditions.

Section A discusses the limitations of the installed RCS instrumentation in distinguishing leakage sources, while Section B describes the system features (such as degasification and venting) that are relevant to monitoring and maintaining RCS integrity, connecting both sections through their focus on instrumentation capabilities for RCS condition monitoring.

Section A describes the design basis and function of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) in removing heat from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), while Section B provides a summary of the RCS's reliance on natural circulation, establishing that the DHRS's effectiveness (A) depends on the natural circulation characteristics described in the RCS summary (B).

Section A describes the design basis and function of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) within the Reactor Coolant System, which is the subject matter introduced in Section B's preface to Chapter Five, thereby providing specific technical details for the general context outlined in Section B.

Section A describes the design basis and function of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) in ensuring safe shutdown by removing decay heat from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), while Section B provides a summary of the RCS's features, including instrumentation relevant to monitoring system conditions, thus Section B's system description supports the adequacy of the instrumentation layout referenced in Section A's safety function.

Section A discusses limitations in RCS leakage detection instrumentation, which impacts the ability to monitor RCS conditions relevant to the DHRS described in Section B, as effective decay heat removal relies on accurate monitoring of RCS parameters during natural circulation.

Section B provides a general overview of the RCS design and its reliance on natural circulation, while Section A specifies inspection and testing requirements for RCS components relevant to ensuring the system's operability, including instrumentation.

Section A discusses inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, while Section B describes the RCS degasification and venting features, both relating to the adequacy of instrumentation and venting for monitoring and maintaining RCS conditions, particularly regarding the removal of noncondensible gases.

Section A provides detailed material specifications for pressurizer components that are generally described as part of the RCS in Section B, connecting the specific pressurizer design to the overall RCS summary.

Section A cites specific sections and material details within the broader document introduced in Section B, establishing a reference from detailed component descriptions to the overall system documentation.
Section A specifies the materials and components of the pressurizer, which is a subsystem described more generally in Section B's summary of the RCS and its functions.

Section B provides references to other sections that describe the materials and components mentioned in Section A, connecting the design and inspection requirements of RCS components to their material specifications.
